coredata.dc:title: Bright tunable multicolor carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes and fingerprint detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yi
Yang, JiQiu
Shi, Wei
Xie, Jianxin
Zhou, Bingshan
Wang, Jiuguang
Zheng, Shihao
Li, Shuguang
Chen, Wenjing
coredata.dc:description: In this study, in order to achieve high quantum yield (QY) and tunable multicolor carbon dots, the influence of quantum size effects and surface state changes were considered comprehensively. Blue-emitting seed carbon dots (S-CDs) were synthesized using 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid and urea as precursors. Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) were then prepared by oxidizing S-CDs with 3 % H2O2, followed by treatment with 0.1 M NaOH to produce strong blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs). Compared with S-CDs, the QY of G-CDs and B-CDs increased by 35 times and 75 times, respectively. The improvement in quantum yield (QY) and the adjustment in emission wavelength were attributed to the disruption of hydrogen bonds and modifications in surface groups caused by treatments with hydrogen peroxide and sodium hydroxide. By combining B-CDs and G-CDs with commercial red phosphors, warm W-LEDs with a color rendering index as high as 91.4 and a correlated color temperature of 5437 K was successfully prepared, which was suitable for indoor lighting. In addition, B-CDs and G-CDs were used as fluorescent markers for latent fingerprints identification on different substrates, the results showed that B-CDs and G-CDs significantly enhanced the resolution of fingerprint imaging. The synthesized G-CDs and B-CDs offer several advantages, including high quantum yield, excellent photostability, low cost and a straightforward synthesis method. These properties provide a solid material foundation for enhancing the performance of white LEDs and detecting latent fingerprints.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tunable multicolor carbon dots
Quantum size effects
Surface state changes
White light-emitting diodes
Latent fingerprint detection

Optical property of the three CDs
The applications of G-CDs and B-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation and application of S-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs.
TEM and HR-TEM images (inset) of (a) S-CDs, (b) G-CDs and (c) B-CDs. Particle size distribution diagrams of (d) S-CDs, (e) G-CDs and (f) B-CDs.
The XPS survey spectra and high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of (a) S-CDs, (b) G-CDs and (c) B-CDs.

UV–visible absorption spectra (black line), FL excitation spectra (red line) and emission spectra (blue line) of (a) S-CDs, (b) G-CDs and (c) B-CDs in aqueous solutions (The inset displays daylight images along with the corresponding optical pictures of the three CDs under 365 nm UV light). FL spectra of (d) S-CDs, (e) G-CDs, (f) B-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
The EL emission spectrum and the optical image of (a) blue, (b) green LED devices and (c) W-LED. (d) CIE values of blue, green, and white LEDs.

Fluorescent images of LFP obtained with G-CDs powder on black cardboard and B-CDs on tin foil by powder spraying method under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, as well as magnified images of characteristic details.

head.ce:title: Bright tunable multicolor carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes and fingerprint detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry Biology and Environment, Yuxi Normal University, Yuxi, 653100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry Biology and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry Biology and Environment, Yuxi Normal University, Yuxi, 653100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiannan Normal University for Nationalites, Duyun, 558000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiannan Normal University for Nationalites, Duyun , 558000 , China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Yuxi Jinhan Printing and Packaging Co., Ltd, Yuxi, 653100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Yuxi Jinhan Printing and Packaging Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Yuxi Jinhan Printing and Packaging Co., Ltd, Yuxi, 653100, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, in order to achieve high quantum yield (QY) and tunable multicolor carbon dots, the influence of quantum size effects and surface state changes were considered comprehensively. Blue-emitting seed carbon dots (S-CDs) were synthesized using 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid and urea as precursors. Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) were then prepared by oxidizing S-CDs with 3 % H2O2, followed by treatment with 0.1 M NaOH to produce strong blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs). Compared with S-CDs, the QY of G-CDs and B-CDs increased by 35 times and 75 times, respectively. The improvement in quantum yield (QY) and the adjustment in emission wavelength were attributed to the disruption of hydrogen bonds and modifications in surface groups caused by treatments with hydrogen peroxide and sodium hydroxide. By combining B-CDs and G-CDs with commercial red phosphors, warm W-LEDs with a color rendering index as high as 91.4 and a correlated color temperature of 5437 K was successfully prepared, which was suitable for indoor lighting. In addition, B-CDs and G-CDs were used as fluorescent markers for latent fingerprints identification on different substrates, the results showed that B-CDs and G-CDs significantly enhanced the resolution of fingerprint imaging. The synthesized G-CDs and B-CDs offer several advantages, including high quantum yield, excellent photostability, low cost and a straightforward synthesis method. These properties provide a solid material foundation for enhancing the performance of white LEDs and detecting latent fingerprints.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs) are widely regarded as excellent lighting devices due to their long operational lifetimes, small size, rapid response, high luminous efficacy, and remarkable energy saving [1,2].As electronic products continue to evolve, concerns about eye strain have grown.W-LEDs with a high color rendering index (CRI) and low correlated color temperature, can help alleviate eye fatigue [3]. Their comfortable ambient light closely mimics natural sunlight, offering a more pleasant visual experience. Currently, a common method for producing white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs) involves combining an InGaN blue LED chip with multicolor phosphors. The commercial phosphors used in W-LEDs primarily include rare-earth-based phosphors and semiconductor quantum dots. Semiconductor quantum dots are known for their high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) [2,4]. However, these quantum dots often contain heavy metals like lead, cadmium, and indium, which pose potential toxicity risks [5]. On the other hand, rare-earth-based phosphors are costly and non-renewable [6]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop multicolor phosphors with excellent fluorescence properties, such as good stability, low cost, low toxicity, and straightforward synthesis methods.

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention due to their notable advantages, including excellent photostability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [1,7]. They hold great promise as conversion layers in W-LEDs because of their high QY [8,9] and tunable fluorescence emission. Additionally, multicolor CDs can also be used in various fields, including fluorescent probes [10,11], LEDs [12–14], bioimaging [15–17], information anti-counterfeiting [18–20] and fingerprint identification [12,21].

Fingerprints are a crucial means of personal identification due to their uniqueness and stability. However, latent fingerprints (LFPs) are often challenging to detect and distinguish at crime scenes. Carbon dots (CDs) can serve as fluorescent markers for LFP identification, reducing background interference and enhancing fingerprint imaging resolution. Thus, preparing multicolor CDs that are simple to synthesize, low in toxicity, and cost-effective is essential for both fingerprint identification and high-performance W-LEDs. Multicolor CDs have been synthesized by varying reaction temperature [22], time [23,24], solvent type [25], or reactant ratios [26]. Nonetheless, preparing multicolor CDs that resist aggregation-induced quenching remains a challenge.The FL property of the solid state multicolor CDs is so fascinating, it is urgent to seek appropriate preparation methods which are easy and moderate.

In this study, blue-emitting seed carbon dots (S-CDs) were synthesized using 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid and urea as precursors, and green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) were successfully prepared by oxidizing S-CDs with 3 % H2O2, then G-CDs were treated with 0.1M NaOH to synthesize strong blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs). The preparation methods of G-CDs and B-CDs were easy, fast and moderate.Using G-CDs and B-CDs as blue and green emitting phosphors, in combination with commercial red emitting phosphor to fabricate a warm WLED. In addition, B-CDs and G-CDs were used as fluorescent markers for latent fingerprint identification on different substrates. The preparation and application of S-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were shown in Fig. 1 .
Materials and Characterization are provided in the electronic supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5253 g of 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid and 0.7525 g of urea were added to 7 mL of deionized water with stirring. The mixture was then heated in a domestic microwave oven at medium-high fire for 10 min. After the reaction, the product was ground in an agate mortar, mixed with 20 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 5 min. The resulting suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles. After drying at 60 °C for 12 h, a white powder was obtained, identified as blue fluorescent S-CDs.

1 g of S-CDs was placed in a beaker, soaked in 10 mL of 3 % H2O2 for 12 h, and then dried at 60 °C. This yielded a milky white powder, which was identified as green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs). The synthesized G-CDs were then soaked in 0.1 M NaOH for 12 h, dried at 60 °C, and the resulting pale yellow powder was identified as strong blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs). The synthesis processes for S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs are illustrated in Fig. S1.

The morphologies of S-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 , S-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were all spherical-like nanoparticles and evenly monodispersed. Their average particle sizes were 31.55 nm, 2.63 nm and 2.01 nm, respectively. Notably, there was a significant difference in size between S-CDs and the other two types. The formation of S-CDs involved cross-linking polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization processes of 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid and urea under microwave heating, resulting in S-CDs with relatively larger sizes [27]. Following oxidation with 3 % H2O2, the surface functional groups of S-CDs were oxidized, leading to increased surface defects and the destruction of some hydrogen bonds. This oxidation resulted in G-CDs with smaller particle sizes compared to S-CDs and caused a red shift in emission wavelength. Subsequent treatment of G-CDs with 0.1M NaOH further disrupted the hydrogen bonds, producing B-CDs with the smallest particle size and resulting in a blue shift in emission wavelength. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed lattice spacings of 0.53 nm, 0.245 nm, and 0.213 nm for S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs, respectively. The lattice spacing of G-CDs matched the (112) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [28], while B-CDs corresponded to the (100) crystal plane [25,29]. As shown in Fig. S2, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for S-CDs and G-CDs displayed a diffraction peak around 27°, indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm, attributed to residual -COOH groups at the edges of the CDs [30,31]. The XRD pattern of B-CDs exhibited multiple sharp diffraction peaks, indicating high crystallinity and abundant surface groups.

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of the three types of carbon dots (CDs) were analyzed using XPS and FTIR. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3 ) reveal that both S-CDs and G-CDs contain C, N, and O elements, with major peaks observed at 285.19 eV, 399.51 eV, and 531.1 eV. The contents of C, N, and O in S-CDs are 54.81 %, 21.39 %, and 23.8 %, respectively, while in G-CDs they are 53.7 %, 20.17 %, and 26.13 %. Compared to S-CDs, G-CDs show a significantly increased oxygen content, indicating higher surface oxidation and more surface defects in G-CDs, which leads to a red shift in emission wavelength. In contrast, the elemental composition of B-CDs is notably different, with Na also being doped. The contents of C (284.8 eV), N (400 eV), O(531 eV) and Na (1071 eV) in B-CDs are 37.21 %, 2.62 %, 41.29 %, and 18.89 %, respectively(Table S1). The significant decrease in nitrogen content may be attributed to NaOH treatment, which likely further disrupts hydrogen bonds and disperses nitrogenous groups on the surface of B-CDs, resulting in a significant reduction in both N content and particle size. These results are consistent with the TEM observations.

The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 3d and e) show that S-CDs and G-CDs have similar surface functional groups, featuring four main peaks corresponding to -COOH (289.16 eV), C=O/C=N (288.35 eV), C-O/C-N (285.12 eV) and C=C/C-C (284.79 eV). For B-CDs(Fig. 3f), the C 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to COOR (289.01 eV), C=O/C=N(288.1 eV),C-O/C-N(284.8 eV) and C=C/C-C (284.29 eV) [32], indicating that all three types of CDs are composed of numerous benzene-like ring structures and exhibit a high degree of graphitization. From the high-resolution N 1s spectra of S-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. 3g and h), three sub-peaks are located at 399.3 eV, 400.21 eV and 398.9 eV, attributed to amino N, pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively [33]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 3i) shows three peaks at 399.74 eV for amino N, 400.3 eV for pyrrolic N [34], and 398.02 eV for sodium nitride. The high-resolution O 1s spectra of S-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. 3j and k) are divided into two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.8 eV, corresponding to C=O and C-OH/C-O-C [35]. Further analysis reveals that compared to the S-CDs, the -COOH content of G-CDs is increased from 42.58 % to 45.25 %, revealing a higher degree of oxidation from S-CDs to B-CDs(Table S2). The O 1s spectrum of B-CDs(Fig. 3l) is separated into three peaks at 535.29 eV for COOR, 530.8 eV for C=O, and 530.38 eV for sodium oxide. These results confirm that the surfaces of all three CDs contain functional groups such as -COOH, -OH, and -NH2, which explains their good water solubility.

As shown in Fig. S3, the FT-IR spectra of S-CDs and G-CDs are quite similar. The absorption peaks at 3438 cm−1, 3325 cm−1 and 3063/3030 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H, O-H and aromatic C-H, respectively [36,37]. The absorption peaks located at 1764 cm−1 and 1720/1622 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C=N [38,39]. The characteristic absorption peak of C-O is observed at 1180 cm−1, while peaks at 1454 cm−1, 1256 cm−1 and 1096 cm−1 are due to the bending vibration of C-H [40]. In contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs shows significant differences. A broad absorption peak at around 3412 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H [41]. The absorption peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1611/1586 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C=O and C=N, while the absorption peaks at 1457 cm-1 and 1132 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of -COONa and C-O [42]. The O-H absorption peak at 3225 cm−1 in G-CDs is stronger than that in S-CDs, indicating more surface oxidation and defects in G-CDs, which results in a red shift in emission wavelength. Compared with the S-CDs and G-CDs, the absorption peaks at 1611/1586 cm−1 (C=N) in B-CDs are weaker, which is consistent with the reduced nitrogen content observed in the XPS spectrum. FT-IR spectra reveal that all three types of carbon dots are rich in hydrophilic functional groups and exhibit amide structures, in agreement with the XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of the three CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the three CDs were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a–c, the UV–vis spectra of S-CDs and G-CDs show two obvious absorption peaks at approximately 222 nm and 300 nm, while B-CDs exhibit peaks at 246 nm and 289 nm. The absorption peaks at 222 nm and 246 nm are attributed to the π−π∗ transition of C=C bonds in the aromatic sp2 domain [19,43]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 289 nm and 300 nm correspond to the n−π∗ transitions of C=N/C=O groups [44,45]. Under excitation wavelengths of 334 nm, 350 nm, and 313 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths of S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs were found to be 435 nm, 507 nm, and 431 nm, respectively. In aqueous solutions, S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs appeared almost colorless and transparent in daylight, yet exhibited dark blue, bright green, and blue luminescence under UV light (365 nm), as shown in the insets of Fig. 4a–c. This luminescence corresponds well with the maximum emission wavelengths of the three CDs.

As shown in Fig. 4d–f, the three CDs demonstrated excitation-independent emission spectra. With increasing excitation wavelengths, the positions of the emission peaks for all three CDs remained nearly unchanged, indicating that they possess a single luminescence center. As displayed in Figs. S4–6, at the maximum excitation wavelength, the quantum yields of S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs were found to be 0.51 %, 17.89 %, and 38 %, respectively. Compared to S-CDs, the QYs of G-CDs and B-CDs increased by 35-fold and 75-fold, respectively. This improvement can be attributed to the fact that after treating S-CDs with 3 % H2O2, more emission sites are generated on the surface of carbon core [46]. The enhanced quantum yield of B-CDs may be linked to the presence of sodium ions, which can bind within the pores of the carbon core, forming metal cationic functional groups. This represents a straightforward and effective approach to increasing the absolute quantum yield [25].

As shown in Fig. S7, solid S-CDs and G-CDs exhibit two characteristic absorption peaks at approximately 212 nm and 250–350 nm. The peak at 212 nm corresponds to the π−π∗ transition of C=C in the aromatic ring, while the peaks within the 250–350 nm range are attributed to the n−π∗ transitions of C=N/C=O bonds [47]. The absorption peak of B-CDs around 212 nm is also associated with the π−π∗ transition of C=C in the aromatic ring. Additionally, the double peaks at 264 nm and 294 nm, along with the shoulder peak near 330 nm, correspond to the n−π∗ transitions of C=O/C=N. These results indicate that all three CDs possess large conjugated domain structures.

As illustrated in Fig. S7 (inset), under sunlight, S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs appear as white powder, milky white powder, and pale yellow powder, respectively. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, they emit blue, green, and bright blue fluorescence, respectively. Under excitation wavelengths of 365 nm, 374 nm, and 329 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths of S-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs are 444 nm, 495 nm, and 424 nm, respectively. Furthermore, these three types of CDs show excitation-independent emission spectra in the solid state, which is consistent with their fluorescence properties in the liquid state.

Although the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs remains still unclear, their widespread applications inspire researchers to investigate them further. Currently, the main photoluminescence models for CDs include quantum size effects [26,48], and cross-linking enhanced emission effects [49] and surface state defects [50,51], etc. In this study, both the quantum size effect and surface states effects were considered in their impact on photoluminescence. Compared to S-CDs, the particle size of G-CDs is smaller. According to the quantum size effect, the emission wavelength should exhibit a blue shift, which was not consistent with the experimental results. Meanwhile, it has been reported that surface defects caused by the oxidation of CDs act as exciton trapping centers, leading to fluorescence emission. The increased carboxyl group content and oxidation degree on the surface of G-CDs can result in a red shift in the emission wavelength [44]. Therefore, the changes in surface states during the formation of G-CDs predominantly influence the photoluminescence, causing a red shift in the wavelength compared to S-CDs. In contrast, the nitrogen content of B-CDs was significantly reduced, compared to G-CDs, which may disrupt the nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of carbon core, leading to a reduction in the particle size of B-CDs. Due to the quantum size effect, the wavelength of B-CDs exhibits a blue shift.

Subsequently, the photostability of G-CDs and B-CDs was investigated. G-CDs and B-CDs were continuously irradiated under UV light (365 nm) for 60 min, and their fluorescence intensity was measured every 10 min. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the two carbon dots remained largely unchanged(Fig. S8). Furthermore, after storing G-CDs and B-CDs at room temperature for 6 months, their fluorescence performance remained stable. This indicates that both types of carbon dots exhibit good photostability and are suitable for use in W-LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The applications of G-CDs and B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent optical properties of G-CDs and B-CDs, they were applied in the preparation of W-LEDs and for potential fingerprint detection. Before fabricating the LED devices, the CDs were carefully ground in an agate mortar until they were very fine. The prepared B-CDs and G-CDs were selected as phosphors in combined with 365 nm UV chips to prepare blue (B-LED) and green (G-LED) LEDs. The CIE coordinates for B-LED and G-LED were (0.21,0.27) and (0.27,0.50), respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 . The correlated color temperature (CCT) values of B-LED and G-LED were 20207K and 7001K and the CRI values were 68.4 and 64.9, respectively. By adding commercial red phosphor and adjusting the ratios of the three phosphors, a W-LED was obtained by encapsulating B-CDs, G-CDs, and red phosphor on a 365 nm UV chip in a ratio of 2:3:1.

At an operating voltage of 3.29 V, the device emits warm white light with a CCT of 5437 K, a CIE coordinate of (0.33, 0.35), and a CRI value of 91.4. The fluorescence spectrum of the prepared W-LED is shown in Fig. 5c, the FL emission peaks around 470 nm and 520 nm correspond to B-CDs and G-CDs, respectively, while FL the emission peak around 605 nm is due to the commercial red phosphor. The fluorescence spectrum covers the entire visible light range from 380 nm to 780 nm, indicating that the prepared W-LED has a wide color gamut. A wide color gamut lighting system can more accurately restore the colors of objects and provide a richer visual experience [51]. The prepared W-LED provides a more comfortable lighting environment, reduces visual fatigue, and meets indoor lighting requirements, due to its relatively uniform spectral distribution. The prepared W-LED exhibits excellent optical performance, and holds broad application prospects in the field of solid-state lighting.

Fingerprints were successfully detected on black cardboard and tin foil using G-CDs and B-CDs as fluorescent markers. As shown in Fig. 6 , the latent fingerprint images obtained with G-CDs and B-CDs under 365 nm UV light were not only vivid and bright, but also revealed fine details clearly, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of fingerprint identification. This significant advantage was mainly attributed to the nanoscale size and lipophilicity of G-CDs and B-CDs. Nanoscale particles allows them to penetrate the fine details of the fingerprint ridges, while their lipophilic nature enhances the interaction between fluorescent markers and the oily components in the fingerprint, thereby increasing the adhesion of the fluorescent markers to the fingerprint [52–54]. In addition, G-CDs and B-CDs, as fluorescent markers, have the advantages of high brightness, good selectivity and excellent contrast, and play a key role in fingerprint detection, greatly improving the accuracy and reliability of potential fingerprint detection.

In this study, solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with tunable multicolor FL and high QY were synthesized by adjusting the particle size and surface state of CDs. G-CDs were successfully prepared by oxidizing S-CDs, and high-brightness B-CDs were successfully synthesized by treating G-CDs with 0.1 M NaOH, Compared with S-CDs, the QY of G-CDs and B-CDs increased by 35 times and 75 times, respectively. The synthesized G-CDs and B-CDs offer advantages such as simple and rapid synthesis method, high quantum yield, good photostability, low cost, low toxicity, etc. They have been successfully used in the preparation of WLEDs with high color rendering index and latent fingerprint detection. This study provides a new insights into synthesizing solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with tunable multicolor FL and high QY, and offers new methods for developing high-performance optoelectronic devices and high-accuracy fluorescent markers.

Yi Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. JiQiu Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Wei Shi: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jianxin Xie: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Bingshan Zhou: Validation, Investigation. Jiuguang Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Shihao Zheng: Validation, Investigation. Shuguang Li: Validation. Wenjing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The manuscript has not been published before and is not being considered for publication elsewhere. All authors have contributed to the creation of this manuscript for important intellectual content and read and approved the final manuscript. We declare there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21465024), Yunnan Province's first-class construction discipline - "New Discipline Cultivation Plan" Plateau Agricultural Ecological Protection and Restoration, the Local Undergraduate Colleges and Universities in Yunnan province (part) joint specific fund project (No. 202311390002), the Scientific Research Foundation of the Education Department of Yunnan Province (No.2024J0883), the Program for Innovation Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Yunnan Province, Special Research Project for High-Level Talents of Qiannan Normal University for Nationalities (No. qnsyrc202201,2024zdzk14) and "Sulfur Phosphorus Titanium" Resource Comprehensive Utilization Engineering Research Center of Guizhou Province (Qian Jiao Ji[2023]046).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2024.121007.
